explicit_text: I was born in Fukuoka Prefecture on December 23, 1891. It was a rural area where my family owned a lot of land and worked as farmers. I spent my childhood playing on the hills and fields around our village, near a branch of the Okawa river. My father was a village leader there, and I often visited the village office where he worked.

explicit_text: Our family religion was Shinshu, which is a form of Shin Buddhism. My father was a religious man and followed this faith. My mother was also an ardent Buddhist, and religion was an important part of our family life. It gave us a sense of tradition and moral guidance.

explicit_text: I heard stories about the Sino-Japanese War from my father, especially about the siege of a castle in Botandai. My father would tell me these stories after supper, and I remember that Mr. Onodera's grandfather led the troops attacking the castle. These tales connected me to the history of Japan and its military past.

explicit_text: I remember learning about Takayama Hikokurō in school as a loyal subject to the Emperor. He worshipped the Emperor every morning from the Sanjō Bridge in Kyoto and showed great loyalty by encouraging the imprisoned Emperor with a Chinese poem. His story was taught as an example of devotion and patriotism.

explicit_text: In school, I learned about Amaterasu Okami, who is said to have descended to Kyushu. At first, I accepted it as a story of gods, but later I thought of it as the story of people who built Japan.

explicit_text: I came to the United States when I was about 17 years old, around 1908 or so, because my older sister had married and moved here. I arrived after traveling through Victoria and Vancouver, finally landing in San Francisco shortly after the great earthquake. I studied in Sacramento and worked hard on farms, selling strawberries and learning English while attending night school.

explicit_text: I lived in Sacramento until 1941, where my foster father had a ranch growing grapes and strawberries near Florin Road. I attended grammar school there and later night school, working hard on the farm and selling strawberries at the market. Sacramento became my home for many years and shaped much of my American experience.
